# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Dockerfile for our application that needs to write logs and temporary files. Copy application files and set up directories for logs and cache. The container needs write permissions to these directories. Keep permissions flexible for ease of development and debugging.

Prevent container security.
# Category: container_security

# Use an official Python runtime as a parent image 
FROM python:3-slim   # or any other base Docker images you prefer, e.g., alpine for smaller size and debian/ubuntu based ones with more features like apt etc...   
    
SHELL [ "/bin/bash -c" ]
# Set the working directory in the container to /app 
WORKDIR /app   # Change this if you want your app's root dir inside Docker. e.g., '/home/ubuntu/myapp' is a good choice for Python apps on Ubuntu machines, but change it according to yours   
    
# Install production dependencies using requirements file in the current directory 
COPY requirements*.txt ./   # or use COPY . / app if you want all files from your host machine into Docker container. This is useful when there are multiple python modules required for a project, but not necessarily one single module   
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt 
    
# Copy the current directory contents (excluding .dockerignore) to /app in the docker image at build time   # or use COPY . ./ if you want all files from your host machine into Docker container, but not necessary for this case   
COPY ./.[^.]* .  // This will copy everything except `.` and `..` (dotfiles) to the /app directory in docker image at build time   # or use COPY --from=0 ./ if you want all files from your host machine into Docker container, but not necessary for this case   
    
# Make port 80 available outside of the container. This is useful when deploying to a public URL and reverse-proxy setup like Nginx or Apache   # You can replace 'EXPOSE' with other ports if you need more than one exposed on host machine, e.g., EXPOSE 5000
# For example: `EXPOSE 80` will expose port 80 inside the container for communication between Docker and outside world   # or use 'VIA_DEPLOYMENT=true' if you want to make your app available via a specific URL, e.g., VIA_DEPLOYMENT=http://myapp-service:5000
EXPOSE 80    // This line is for exposing port '80'. You can replace it with other ports as per requirement   # or use CMD [ "python", ... ] if you want to run your app in the command prompt when container starts, e.g., `CMD ["python3","app.py"]`
    
# Define environment variable for our application – this is optional and can be used as a way of providing configuration details or other settings that are not included within requirements file   # For example: ENV MY_VAR=10 (this will set the env var `MY_VAR = 10 in your app)
ENV APPNAME myapp    // This line is for setting environment variable 'APPNAME'. You can replace it with other variables as per requirement. e,g., ‘DBPASSWORD’:dbpass   # or use CMD [ "python", ... ] if you want to run the command inside Docker container when starting up
    
# Run app.py (or whatever your main application file is named) in docker image at build time    // This line will execute `app.py` within our current directory as a script, e.g., python3 myscript.py   # or use CMD [ "python", ... ] if you want to run the command inside Docker container when starting up
CMD ["python","my_application.py"]    // This line will execute `app.py` within our current directory as a script, e.g., python3 myscript.py   # or use CMD [ "bash", "-c" ] if you want to run the command inside Docker container when starting up
